input:
# Urban Mobility Survey: Annual Report

## About This Report

This report presents findings from a one-year survey of commuting patterns across three districts.

## Survey Design

The study deployed monitoring equipment at 26 stations. Figure 1 maps the coverage area and station locations.

**Figure 1: Survey coverage area and monitoring station locations**

| District | Stations | Coverage (km²) |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| North    | 8        | 45             |
| Central  | 12       | 32             |
| South    | 6        | 28             |

## Commute Time Results

Commute durations fell across all districts following the intervention. Table 1 summarises average commute times before and after.

**Table 1: Average commute times before and after intervention (minutes)**

| District | Before | After | Change |
|----------|--------|-------|--------|
| North    | 34     | 29    | −15%   |
| Central  | 42     | 36    | −14%   |
| South    | 28     | 25    | −11%   |

## Modal Shift Results

Modal shift was the primary driver of the observed time savings. Figure 2 shows the change in modal share by district.

**Figure 2: Modal share change by district (percentage points)**

| District | Car | Transit | Cycling | Walking |
|----------|-----|---------|---------|---------|
| North    | −8  | +5      | +2      | +1      |
| Central  | −10 | +6      | +3      | +1      |
| South    | −6  | +4      | +1      | +1      |

## Conclusion

Investment in transit infrastructure reduced car dependency across all three districts.
target_answer: The document has two figures (Figure 1, Figure 2) and one table (Table 1). All three have descriptive titles, are numbered sequentially, and all body references resolve to elements that exist in the document. The agent should report zero issues.

input:
# Quarterly Sales Performance Report

## Revenue by Product Line

Figure 1 provides a breakdown of revenue by product line for Q3 2024.

**Figure 1: Revenue by product line (Q3 2024, USD thousands)**

| Product Line | Q3 2023 | Q3 2024 | Change |
|--------------|---------|---------|--------|
| Hardware     | 1,240   | 1,380   | +11%   |
| Software     | 890     | 1,050   | +18%   |
| Services     | 460     | 430     | −7%    |

## Growth Rate Trend

Figure 2 illustrates the trend in year-over-year growth rates since Q1 2023. The software segment has consistently outperformed the other lines since Q2 2024.

Figure 2:

| Quarter | Hardware | Software | Services |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|
| Q1 2023 | +4%      | +6%      | +2%      |
| Q2 2023 | +5%      | +9%      | +1%      |
| Q3 2023 | +6%      | +12%     | −1%      |
| Q1 2024 | +8%      | +15%     | −3%      |
| Q2 2024 | +9%      | +17%     | −5%      |
| Q3 2024 | +11%     | +18%     | −7%      |

## Conclusion

Software continues to be the primary growth driver. Services should be reviewed for possible consolidation.
target_answer: Figure 1 has a proper descriptive title. Figure 2 is labelled only as 'Figure 2:' with no descriptive title or caption following the label — the label appears alone on a line with nothing after the colon. The agent should report exactly one issue: 'Figure/Table Missing Title: Figure 2'.

input:
# Biodiversity Survey: Wetland Species Inventory

## Study Sites

Four wetland sites were selected for monitoring. Figure 1 maps the study sites with their coordinates and areas.

**Figure 1: Map of the four wetland study sites**

| Site | Latitude  | Longitude  | Area (ha) |
|------|-----------|------------|-----------|
| W1   | 51.42° N  | 0.18° W    | 32        |
| W2   | 51.39° N  | 0.24° W    | 18        |
| W3   | 51.35° N  | 0.31° W    | 45        |
| W4   | 51.33° N  | 0.27° W    | 27        |

## Species Diversity

Amphibian diversity was highest at sites W1 and W3. Table 1 shows observed species counts by taxonomic group.

**Table 1: Observed species counts by taxonomic group**

| Site | Birds | Amphibians | Invertebrates | Mammals |
|------|-------|------------|---------------|---------|
| W1   | 42    | 9          | 118           | 7       |
| W2   | 31    | 4          | 87            | 4       |
| W3   | 47    | 11         | 134           | 8       |
| W4   | 28    | 5          | 72            | 5       |

## Seasonal Patterns

Seasonal variation in species occurrence was recorded for birds and amphibians.

**Table 2: Seasonal species occurrence index (0–1 scale)**

| Site | Spring | Summer | Autumn | Winter |
|------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| W1   | 0.82   | 0.91   | 0.67   | 0.44   |
| W2   | 0.71   | 0.83   | 0.59   | 0.35   |
| W3   | 0.88   | 0.94   | 0.73   | 0.51   |
| W4   | 0.65   | 0.78   | 0.52   | 0.29   |

## Conclusion

Larger wetland sites consistently support greater species diversity.
target_answer: Figure 1 and Table 1 each have proper titles and are referenced in their respective sections. Table 2 has a descriptive title and is properly numbered, but it is never cited or mentioned anywhere in the body text — the Seasonal Patterns section introduces the section without a callout such as 'see Table 2' or 'as shown in Table 2'. The agent should report exactly one issue: 'Unreferenced Figure/Table: Table 2'.

input:
# Water Quality Assessment: Industrial Zone Study

## Sampling Points

Six sampling points were established downstream of the industrial zone. Table 1 lists their locations and baseline characteristics.

**Table 1: Sampling point locations and baseline characteristics**

| Point | Distance from source (km) | Baseline pH | Baseline DO (mg/L) |
|-------|--------------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| P1    | 0.5                      | 7.1         | 8.4                |
| P2    | 1.2                      | 6.8         | 7.9                |
| P3    | 2.4                      | 6.5         | 7.2                |
| P4    | 4.1                      | 6.9         | 7.6                |
| P5    | 6.3                      | 7.0         | 8.1                |
| P6    | 9.8                      | 7.2         | 8.5                |

## pH Results

Figure 1 shows pH readings at all sampling points across four monitoring months. pH was consistently lowest at point P3 throughout the period.

**Figure 1: pH readings at sampling points over the monitoring period**

| Month | P1  | P2  | P3  | P4  | P5  | P6  |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Jan   | 6.9 | 6.5 | 6.1 | 6.6 | 6.8 | 7.0 |
| Apr   | 7.0 | 6.6 | 6.3 | 6.7 | 6.9 | 7.1 |
| Jul   | 7.1 | 6.7 | 6.4 | 6.8 | 7.0 | 7.2 |
| Oct   | 7.0 | 6.6 | 6.2 | 6.7 | 6.9 | 7.1 |

## Dissolved Oxygen and Heavy Metals

Figure 2 provides a comparison of dissolved oxygen levels across all monitoring months, confirming that the impact zone is concentrated between P2 and P4.

Table 2 details the heavy metal concentrations recorded at each point; all values were below regulatory limits except for lead at P3 in July.

## Conclusion

The data consistently point to a localised pollution source near sampling point P3.
target_answer: Table 1 and Figure 1 exist in the document with proper titles and callouts in their respective sections. The 'Dissolved Oxygen and Heavy Metals' section references Figure 2 and Table 2 but neither exists in the document. The agent should report exactly two issues: 'Missing Figure/Table: Figure 2' and 'Missing Figure/Table: Table 2'.

The RAND biodefense report uses chapter-based numbering (TABLE S.1, FIGURE 2.1, TABLE 3.1, TABLE 3.2, etc.) which is a valid consistent scheme. TABLE 3.1 has a bare label on one line but is immediately followed by the section heading 'Common Interventions to Prevent Vital Worker Incapacitation from Biological Threats', which serves as its title — this is a PDF-to-markdown conversion artefact rather than a genuine missing caption. All figures and tables are referenced in the body text and no body references point to non-existent elements. The agent should report zero issues.

The RAND Europe AGI report has twelve figures (Figure 1–12), all with descriptive titles and sequential numbering. Figures 1–11 are each cited in the body text with an explicit callout. Figure 12 ('The major trade partners of the US in 2024') appears in the document but is never explicitly referenced in the body text — the surrounding text discusses EU trade weight in general terms without a callout such as 'see Figure 12'. Figure 12 is also absent from the table of figures. The agent should report exactly one issue: 'Unreferenced Figure/Table: Figure 12'. Note: Figure 2 lacks an in-body callout but appears in the table of figures; the agent may or may not flag it as unreferenced depending on whether it counts the table of figures as a body reference.

# Renewable Energy Adoption: Regional Overview

## Survey Coverage

The report covers energy adoption trends across four regions. Figure 1 maps the regions included in the survey.

**Figure 1: Survey regions and their geographic boundaries**

| Region   | Area (km²) | Population (millions) |
|----------|------------|----------------------|
| North    | 142,000    | 3.2                  |
| East     | 98,000     | 5.1                  |
| South    | 115,000    | 4.4                  |
| West     | 87,000     | 2.9                  |

## Solar Installation Rates

Solar capacity grew across all regions. Figure 2 shows the annual solar installation rates from 2020 to 2023.

**Figure 2: Annual solar installation rates by region (MW/year)**

| Year | North | East | South | West |
|------|-------|------|-------|------|
| 2020 | 120   | 210  | 180   | 95   |
| 2021 | 145   | 245  | 205   | 110  |
| 2022 | 190   | 290  | 250   | 140  |
| 2023 | 230   | 335  | 295   | 170  |

## Wind Installation Rates

Wind capacity also expanded significantly. Figure 2 shows the annual wind installation rates across the same period.

**Figure 2: Annual wind installation rates by region (MW/year)**

| Year | North | East | South | West |
|------|-------|------|-------|------|
| 2020 | 85    | 60   | 40    | 110  |
| 2021 | 100   | 75   | 55    | 130  |
| 2022 | 125   | 95   | 70    | 160  |
| 2023 | 155   | 120  | 90    | 195  |

## Conclusion

All regions showed sustained growth in renewable energy capacity over the four-year period.
target_answer: The document has three figures but only two distinct numbers — Figure 1 (survey regions) appears once, and Figure 2 is used twice: once for solar installation rates and once for wind installation rates. Both uses of Figure 2 have descriptive titles and are referenced in their respective sections. The agent should report exactly one issue: 'Inconsistent Numbering: Figure 2 appears twice'.

input:
# Urban Air Quality Report: Multi-City Study

## Study Coverage

Four cities were monitored over a six-month period. Figure 1 shows the geographic distribution of monitoring sites across the study area.

**Figure 1: Geographic distribution of air quality monitoring sites**

| City       | Sites | Region    |
|------------|-------|-----------|
| Alderton   | 8     | North     |
| Bracewell  | 6     | East      |
| Castleford | 10    | Central   |
| Dunmere    | 5     | South     |

## Particulate Matter

Particulate matter (PM2.5) levels exceeded safe thresholds on multiple occasions. Figure 3 shows the monthly average PM2.5 concentrations for each city.

**Figure 3: Monthly average PM2.5 concentrations by city (µg/m³)**

| Month | Alderton | Bracewell | Castleford | Dunmere |
|-------|----------|-----------|------------|---------|
| Jan   | 18       | 22        | 31         | 14      |
| Feb   | 16       | 20        | 28         | 12      |
| Mar   | 13       | 17        | 24         | 10      |
| Apr   | 11       | 15        | 21         | 9       |
| May   | 9        | 13        | 18         | 8       |
| Jun   | 8        | 11        | 16         | 7       |

## Nitrogen Dioxide

Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) levels were closely correlated with traffic density. Figure 2 shows NO2 readings at peak and off-peak hours for each city.

**Figure 2: NO2 concentrations at peak and off-peak hours by city (µg/m³)**

| City       | Peak | Off-peak |
|------------|------|----------|
| Alderton   | 48   | 21       |
| Bracewell  | 55   | 24       |
| Castleford | 72   | 31       |
| Dunmere    | 38   | 17       |

## Ozone Levels

Ozone concentrations were highest during warm months. Figure 4 shows the seasonal ozone index for each city.

**Figure 4: Seasonal ozone index by city (0–100 scale)**

| City       | Winter | Spring | Summer | Autumn |
|------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Alderton   | 22     | 41     | 68     | 35     |
| Bracewell  | 25     | 44     | 73     | 38     |
| Castleford | 30     | 52     | 81     | 45     |
| Dunmere    | 19     | 37     | 62     | 31     |

## Conclusion

Castleford consistently recorded the highest pollutant levels across all three measures.
target_issue_titles: Inconsistent Numbering: Figures are out of order (Figure 3 appears before Figure 2)
target_answer: The document has four figures (Figure 1, Figure 2, Figure 3, Figure 4), all with descriptive titles and all referenced in their respective sections. All four numbers are present with no gaps. However, the figures do not appear in numerical order — Figure 3 (PM2.5 concentrations) appears in the document before Figure 2 (NO2 concentrations). The agent should report exactly one issue: 'Inconsistent Numbering: Figures are out of order (Figure 3 appears before Figure 2)'.

# Coastal Erosion Study: Annual Monitoring Report

## Study Area

The study monitored three coastal sites over a 12-month period. Figure 1 shows the locations of all monitoring stations along the shoreline.

**Figure 1: Monitoring station locations along the study coastline**

| Station | Latitude | Longitude | Elevation (m) |
|---------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| S1      | 50.71° N | 1.88° W   | 4             |
| S2      | 50.68° N | 1.93° W   | 6             |
| S3      | 50.65° N | 1.97° W   | 3             |

## Erosion Rates

Annual erosion rates were measured at each station. Figure 2 summarises the observed erosion rates across the monitoring period.

**Figure 2: Annual erosion rates by station (mm/year)**

| Station | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|
| S1      | 42     | 38     | 45     |
| S2      | 61     | 57     | 63     |
| S3      | 29     | 31     | 27     |

## Wave Energy

Wave energy flux was the primary driver of erosion at all three stations. Figure 4 shows the monthly wave energy flux recorded at each station throughout the study period.

**Figure 4: Monthly wave energy flux by station (kW/m)**

| Month | S1  | S2  | S3  |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|
| Jan   | 28  | 31  | 22  |
| Apr   | 19  | 21  | 15  |
| Jul   | 12  | 14  | 9   |
| Oct   | 24  | 27  | 18  |

## Conclusion

Station S2 consistently exhibited the highest erosion rates, driven by elevated wave energy exposure.
target_issue_titles: Inconsistent Numbering: Figure numbering skips from Figure 2 to Figure 4
target_answer: The document has three figures (Figure 1, Figure 2, Figure 4), all with descriptive titles and all referenced in the body text. However, Figure 3 is absent — the numbering jumps from Figure 2 to Figure 4 with no Figure 3 anywhere in the document. The agent should report exactly one issue: 'Inconsistent Numbering: Figure numbering skips from Figure 2 to Figure 4'.

# Regional Healthcare Infrastructure Report

## Facility Locations

The study covers 42 public health facilities across four districts. Figure 1 maps the facility locations and their service catchment areas.

**Figure 1: Public health facility locations and catchment areas**

| District | Facilities | Population served |
|----------|------------|-------------------|
| North    | 11         | 182,000           |
| East     | 9          | 154,000           |
| South    | 14         | 231,000           |
| West     | 8          | 127,000           |

## Staffing Levels

Staffing shortfalls were identified in all four districts. Figure 2 shows the ratio of clinical staff to population across districts.

**Figure 2**

| District | Doctors per 10,000 | Nurses per 10,000 |
|----------|--------------------|-------------------|
| North    | 8.2                | 24.1              |
| East     | 6.7                | 19.4              |
| South    | 7.1                | 21.8              |
| West     | 5.9                | 17.2              |

## Equipment Inventory

Equipment availability was assessed against WHO minimum standards. Table 1 summarises the inventory findings by district.

**Table 1: Medical equipment availability against WHO minimums (%)**

| Equipment Type    | North | East | South | West |
|-------------------|-------|------|-------|------|
| Diagnostic imaging | 91   | 74   | 88    | 61   |
| Surgical equipment | 85   | 68   | 82    | 57   |
| Emergency supplies | 96   | 83   | 94    | 72   |

## Patient Outcomes

**Table 2: Patient outcome indicators by district**

| Indicator                  | North | East | South | West |
|----------------------------|-------|------|-------|------|
| 30-day readmission rate (%) | 8.1  | 11.4 | 9.2   | 13.7 |
| Average length of stay (days) | 4.2 | 5.8 | 4.7  | 6.3  |
| Patient satisfaction score  | 78   | 65   | 74    | 59   |

## Funding Allocation

Figure 3 details the breakdown of capital funding allocated to each district over the review period.

## Conclusion

Western districts show the greatest infrastructure deficit and should be prioritised in the next funding cycle.
target_answer: The document has three figures and two tables. Figure 1 and Table 1 are properly titled and referenced. Figure 2 has a bare '**Figure 2**' label with no descriptive title. Table 2 has a descriptive title but is never cited in the body text — the Patient Outcomes section introduces it with no callout. The Funding Allocation section references Figure 3 but no Figure 3 exists anywhere in the document. The agent should report exactly three issues: 'Figure/Table Missing Title: Figure 2', 'Unreferenced Figure/Table: Table 2', and 'Missing Figure/Table: Figure 3'.

input:
# Climate Policy Brief: Summary of Key Findings

## Overview

This brief summarises the main findings from a review of climate policy instruments across twelve countries. The review focused on carbon pricing mechanisms, renewable energy subsidies, and emissions trading schemes.

## Carbon Pricing

Carbon pricing was the most widely adopted instrument among the countries reviewed. Ten of the twelve countries had implemented some form of carbon tax or emissions trading scheme by 2023. Prices ranged from under five dollars per tonne to over one hundred dollars per tonne, with significant variation driven by political and economic factors.

## Renewable Energy Subsidies

Renewable energy subsidies were present in all twelve countries, though their structure varied considerably. Feed-in tariffs were more common in European countries, while tax credits dominated in North America. Several countries had begun phasing out subsidies as renewable energy reached cost parity with fossil fuels.

## Emissions Trading

Emissions trading schemes were operational in eight of the twelve countries. Coverage varied from economy-wide schemes to sector-specific programmes targeting electricity generation and heavy industry. Linking schemes across borders remained limited, with only two cross-border programmes in operation.

## Conclusion

Carbon pricing and renewable subsidies remain the primary policy levers for emissions reduction. Greater international coordination on emissions trading could improve efficiency and reduce carbon leakage.
target_answer: The document contains no figures or tables. There are no labels, captions, or body-text references to any figure or table anywhere in the document. The agent should report zero issues.

input:
# Soil Contamination Survey: Site Assessment Report

## Site Overview

The survey assessed contamination levels at a former industrial site. Soil samples were collected at 20 locations across the site perimeter and interior. Table 1 lists the sample locations and their coordinates.

**Table 1: Sample locations and coordinates**

| Sample ID | Easting | Northing | Zone     |
|-----------|---------|----------|----------|
| S01       | 412840  | 287340   | Perimeter|
| S02       | 412910  | 287280   | Perimeter|
| S03       | 412760  | 287190   | Interior |
| S04       | 412830  | 287150   | Interior |
| S05       | 412950  | 287210   | Interior |

## Contamination Levels

Samples were analysed for heavy metal concentrations. The results are shown below.

| Sample ID | Lead (mg/kg) | Arsenic (mg/kg) | Cadmium (mg/kg) |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|------------------|
| S01       | 142         | 18              | 3.2              |
| S02       | 87          | 11              | 1.8              |
| S03       | 310         | 34              | 6.7              |
| S04       | 65          | 8               | 1.1              |
| S05       | 198         | 22              | 4.4              |

## Remediation Thresholds

All lead and arsenic values at sample S03 exceeded regulatory remediation thresholds. Cadmium levels remained below thresholds at all locations.

## Conclusion

Sample location S03 requires immediate remediation. The remaining locations fall within acceptable limits for commercial land use.
target_answer: The document contains two tables. Table 1 is properly labeled, titled, and referenced in the body text. The second table in the Contamination Levels section has no label or title — it begins directly with the markdown table row with no preceding 'Table X' label or caption, and the body text refers to it only as 'the results' with no numbered callout. The agent should report exactly one issue: 'Figure/Table Missing Title: Unlabeled table'.

input:
# Network Security Assessment Report

## Executive Summary

This report presents findings from the annual network security assessment conducted across all regional offices.

## Vulnerability Scan Results

Table 1 summarises the number of vulnerabilities discovered by severity level.

**Table 1: Vulnerabilities by severity level**

| Severity | Count | Remediated | Remaining |
|----------|-------|------------|-----------|
| Critical | 3     | 3          | 0         |
| High     | 12    | 10         | 2         |
| Medium   | 28    | 19         | 9         |
| Low      | 47    | 31         | 16        |

## Penetration Test Results

Figure 1 shows the attack paths identified during the penetration test.

**Figure 1: Attack paths identified during penetration testing**

| Attack Path         | Entry Point     | Impact   | Exploited |
|---------------------|-----------------|----------|-----------|
| Phishing → Lateral  | Email gateway   | High     | Yes       |
| SQL Injection       | Web application | Critical | Yes       |
| Default Credentials | IoT devices     | Medium   | Yes       |

## Abbreviations

| Abbreviation | Definition |
|--------------|------------|
| SQL          | Structured Query Language |
| IoT          | Internet of Things |
| VPN          | Virtual Private Network |
| IDS          | Intrusion Detection System |
| SIEM         | Security Information and Event Management |
| MFA          | Multi-Factor Authentication |
| CVE          | Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures |

## Conclusion

The overall security posture has improved compared to the previous assessment cycle.
target_answer: The document has one table (Table 1) and one figure (Figure 1), both with proper titles and body-text references. The Abbreviations section contains a two-column table mapping abbreviations to their definitions — this is a standard abbreviation/acronym table and must be excluded from all figures-and-tables rules. It should not be flagged for missing a title, number, or body-text reference. The agent should report zero issues.

input:
# Annual Climate Monitoring Report

## Overview

This report analyses temperature and precipitation trends across five monitoring stations over a three-year period.

## Temperature Trends

Figure 1 shows the annual mean temperature at each station.

**Figure 1: Annual mean temperature by station (°C)**

| Station | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 |
|---------|------|------|------|
| Alpha   | 14.2 | 14.8 | 15.1 |
| Beta    | 12.7 | 13.1 | 13.5 |
| Gamma   | 16.3 | 16.9 | 17.2 |
| Delta   | 11.8 | 12.3 | 12.6 |
| Epsilon | 15.0 | 15.5 | 15.9 |

## Precipitation Patterns

Total annual precipitation varied significantly between stations. Table 1 provides the annual totals.

**Table 1: Annual total precipitation by station (mm)**

| Station | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 |
|---------|------|------|------|
| Alpha   | 820  | 790  | 845  |
| Beta    | 1,120| 1,080| 1,150|
| Gamma   | 640  | 610  | 670  |
| Delta   | 950  | 920  | 980  |
| Epsilon | 740  | 710  | 760  |

## Extreme Events

Figure 3 details the number of extreme weather events recorded at each station.

**Figure 3: Extreme weather events by station and year**

| Station | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 |
|---------|------|------|------|
| Alpha   | 4    | 6    | 8    |
| Beta    | 7    | 9    | 11   |
| Gamma   | 2    | 3    | 5    |
| Delta   | 5    | 7    | 9    |
| Epsilon | 3    | 5    | 7    |

## List of Abbreviations

| Abbreviation | Description |
|--------------|-------------|
| NOAA | National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration |
| WMO  | World Meteorological Organization |
| IPCC | Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change |
| GHG  | Greenhouse Gas |
| SST  | Sea Surface Temperature |

## Conclusion

All five stations show a consistent warming trend over the three-year period.
target_issue_titles: Inconsistent Numbering: Figure numbering skips from Figure 1 to Figure 3
target_answer: The document has two figures (Figure 1, Figure 3) and one table (Table 1), all with proper titles and body-text references. However, figure numbering skips from Figure 1 to Figure 3 with no Figure 2 anywhere in the document. The List of Abbreviations section contains a two-column table mapping abbreviations to descriptions — this is a standard abbreviation table and must be excluded from all figures-and-tables rules (it should not be flagged for missing title, number, or body-text reference). The agent should report exactly one issue: 'Inconsistent Numbering: Figure numbering skips from Figure 1 to Figure 3'.
